summary: Gruber challenges the European Commission's antitrust objections against Apple over NFC access and Apple Pay, arguing that Apple created a thriving contactless payment market from near-nothing through superior design and integration. He points out that NFC payments barely existed before Apple Pay despite Android supporting them years earlier, and that Apple Pay's 92% U.S. market share reflects better execution, not anticompetitive behavior. He walks through the practical UX and security implications of opening NFC to third-party wallet apps, showing how each scenario would degrade the experience. He concludes that the E.C. hasn't identified a concrete aggrieved party or articulated what Apple should have done differently.
key_points: Apple Pay succeeded because of superior design and integration, not because Apple blocked competitors — Android had NFC payments for years before Apple Pay launched and still trails badly
The E.C. dismisses legitimate security concerns about third-party NFC access without providing evidence or a concrete technical alternative
Opening the hardware side button and NFC stack to third-party wallets would create UX confusion and security risks that the E.C. hasn't addressed
Apple's new Tap to Pay on iPhone already supports other digital wallets, undermining the claim that Apple blocks NFC competition
The wallet itself belongs as part of the OS; the cards inside it are already open to third parties, which is the appropriate boundary
money_quotes: Apple took a vibrant, perfectly balanced market where NFC payments were used by almost no one and turned it into a market where Apple Pay is accepted at most brick and mortar retailers and millions of iPhone users enjoy using it.
CurrentC seems to be the sort of thing the E.C. is seeking to prop up — user-hostile but mid-sized-company-friendly.
Translation: Apple designs, implements, and controls iOS. Amazing scoop from the E.C.'s crack investigation team.
You know, your classic three-way neck-and-neck horse race.
This dismissiveness on the part of the E.C. is infuriatingly facile.

Is it limiting that Apple Wallet is the only wallet on iOS? Yes. Is it simpler and easier to understand that way? Yes. Is that why Apple Pay dominates the contactless payment market worldwide, despite Android having supported NFC payments years before iOS did and having far larger market share in handset sales? I say yes, undoubtedly.
Whose third-party wallet app is being stomped upon by iOS today? Who's the aggrieved party here?
What, exactly, should Apple have done differently that would have appeased the E.C.? I genuinely can't come up with an answer for this.
key_insight: Apple Pay's market dominance stems from superior design and deep system integration, not anticompetitive behavior, and the E.C.'s demand to open NFC to third parties lacks both a concrete technical proposal and an identifiable harmed party.
